data.title: Why Investors Are Turning Bullish On Adeia: The Bull Case Explained
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The broader technology market has been under pressure due to tariff uncertainty, macroeconomic concerns, and rising rates. Adeia Inc. (ADEA) has done the opposite. The stock has increased by ~40% in the last three months, while the Nasdaq has declined by 10%. Isn't this worth paying attention to? This kind of outperformance does not happen without a good reason. In this case, the company has been signing multi-year IP licensing deals with some of the largest technology companies in the world.

In March 2026, Adeia signed a multi-year IP license agreement with Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). This followed the January 2026 multi-year agreement with Microsoft (MSFT) and the December 2025 long-term Media IP License deal with The Walt Disney Company (DIS). Each of these deals has two important characteristics that investors should consider.

First, all three are multi-year agreements. The exact duration is not disclosed, but in my understanding, multi-year typically means anywhere between three and ten years. In a nutshell, these deals will generate predictable recurring revenue for several years. Second, all three are with Tier-1 technology companies. That validates the quality and relevance of Adeia's patent portfolio in both the media and semiconductor industries.

Adeia is not a typical technology stock that makes products or sells software subscriptions. The company licenses its patent portfolio to companies operating in media technology, semiconductor design, and consumer electronics. The company was formed in October 2022 following the separation of Xperi Inc. (XPER).

Tier-1 Tech Deals: Disney, Microsoft, AMD, and What They Mean

Adeia signed a long-term IP license agreement with Disney for its media technology portfolio. This deal covers IP for media streaming and content delivery technology. With Microsoft, the company signed a multi-year IP license agreement covering the media portfolio and potentially semiconductors as well. The Microsoft deal was announced alongside the appointment of Craig Mitchell as Chief Semiconductor Officer and other key leadership additions. In my view, this is a clear signal that the company is looking for growth in the semiconductor sector. To support this, the company also signed a multi-year agreement with AMD.

For investors, it's important to gauge the impact of these deals on the fundamentals of the company and what connects these three deals. All three were signed in a span of a few months. Further, all are multi-year agreements with the capability to generate predictable recurring revenue. And most importantly, they are with companies that operate at the core of the technology ecosystem through their product offerings in AI compute, streaming media, and semiconductor design.

CEO Paul Davis confirmed in the Q4 2025 earnings call: "Our continued progress on diversifying our revenue base and expanding our customer reach in growth markets positions us well for the years ahead."

As per my analysis, these deals indicate a broader trend of licensing activity for the company, which is confirmed by the 13% growth in the patent portfolio for FY 2025. A validation by the larger companies confirmed the relevance of Adeia's patent portfolio, which will create more opportunities for licensing and a potential for upside.

AMD Deal and Semiconductor Pivot: The Most Important Near-Term Catalyst

As I have highlighted, in January 2026, the company expanded its leadership team to accelerate growth in semiconductors. In March 2026, the company finalized the multi-year deal with AMD, which signifies a strategy to focus on the semiconductor sector. This is the most important development in the Adeia story right now.

In November 2025, Adeia filed ten patent infringement lawsuits against AMD in the US District Court for the Western District of Texas. The patents covered semiconductor innovations in hybrid bonding technology and advanced process node technology. These are technologies that are directly relevant to how AI chips are designed and manufactured.

For years, AMD’s products have incorporated and made extensive use of Adeia’s patented semiconductor innovations, which have greatly contributed to their success as a market leader. After prolonged efforts to reach a mutually agreeable resolution without litigation, we believe this step was necessary to defend our intellectual property from AMD’s continued unauthorized use." said CEO Paul Davis.

Interestingly, in the span of a few months of the lawsuit being filed, AMD signed a multi-year IP license agreement with Adeia. As per my analysis, this is the strong indicator of patent validity. It strengthens Adeia's position in every future semiconductor licensing conversation with new customers.

The semiconductor licensing business at Adeia is still small. But it is growing fast, and the trajectory is compelling.

Data in $million FY2023 FY2024 FY2025
Revenue 13 18 26
Growth 38% 40%

Compiled by the author using data from Earnings Transcript Q4 2025.

Semiconductor revenue grew 40% in FY 2025 to $26 million from $18 million in FY 2024. Going forward, the recurring revenue from deals with AMD and Microsoft will be added to revenue. The reason for bringing up this data point is to highlight the potential of Adeia as a part of emerging technologies of the future and as the growth engine investors should track over the next 2–3 years.

IP Licensing Market: A Growing Addressable Opportunity

Adeia operates in the global IP licensing market, which will grow as technological innovation accelerates globally. According to Strategic Market Research, the global IP licensing market is estimated to increase from $13.9 billion in 2024 to $21.6 billion by 2030, at a CAGR of 7.5%. The growth drivers are structural, where AI drives new IP categories, and this will create new IP licensing opportunities. The semiconductor industry requires specialized process patents, and Adeia's IP patent portfolio is expanding. In a nutshell, Adeia's patent portfolio covers all booming sectors, i.e., streaming media, AI semiconductors, and consumer electronics. Investors should consider this point while evaluating this company.

Still Trading at a Discount Despite the Rally

Despite the rally in the last 3 months, it still trades at 16.10x forward P/E on FY 2026 EPS, a 22% discount to the sector median of 20.39x. InterDigital (IDCC) is the most direct comparable peer, trading at 29.91x. At that multiple on FY 2026 EPS of $1.44, the implied stock price will be $43.25 against the current price of $23.13. Given the multiple deals the company has completed in a few months, I believe the stock deserves the premium compared to its peers.

Final Thoughts

The company is signing multi-year deals with Disney, Microsoft, and now AMD. Further, the patent portfolio is growing at 13% annually. In my view, the stock is cheap in comparison to its growth potential in media and semiconductors. In a nutshell, this is a high-margin business with a growing patent portfolio and a record of new customer trading at a discount. Investors should watch Q1 2026 results closely for the contribution from the AMD deal.

data.title: Diversification By Design: Tapping The Return Potential Of Everyday Cash Flows
data.description:
By James Sackett & Neil Hepworth

Curating diverse return streams is important. Asset-based finance may help.

Diversification is often treated as an asset-allocation decision. How much capital should go to equities? Bonds? Alternatives? But portfolio design matters, too, and this is where we think asset-based finance can play a crucial role.

Rather than relying on return correlations among traditional investment categories, asset-based finance seeks to generate returns by securitizing the cash flows from a broad array of income-generating assets backed by diverse collateral. These include auto loans, mortgages and other forms of consumer debt.

Investments are secured by hundreds—and sometimes thousands—of loans and other tangible, income-generating assets that produce contractual cash flows according to different timelines. And because the loans are self-amortizing, they repay principal gradually over time, becoming less risky as they do.

That’s distinct from the way corporate loans typically work: they pay regular interest but don’t repay any principal until the end of the loan term.

As we see it, these features may help to generate steady returns at a time when late-cycle dynamics and AI-related anxiety threaten to push investors into making decisions based on fear, not fundamentals.

Asset-Based Finance’s Origin Story

In the past, borrowers turned to banks for this type of financing. But tighter regulations have pushed much of that activity to specialty finance companies. These nonbank lenders don’t have balance sheets big enough to hold all the loans they originate, so they partner with private credit investors.

In practice, that means specialty lenders source, originate and service the loans, while private credit investors set the parameters for borrowers and collateral types, provide the capital and own the loans.

There is more than $6 trillion of debt in the specialty finance ecosystem today, making it the primary source of lending for the real economy. We expect the size of the market to exceed $10 trillion before the end of the decade—an increase that has yet to be fully reflected in investor allocations.

Diversified Sources and Investor-Friendly Structuring

The breadth of cash flows and the backing collateral are key strengths, in our view. Residential mortgages, consumer installment loans, point-of-sale finance and other contractual payment streams all generate income from different sources, over different time horizons and with different sensitivities to economic conditions.

What’s more, asset-based loan portfolios can be structured to be “bankruptcy remote.” This means they’re not tied to a company’s financial performance, including the risk of corporate default (Display).

The Nuts and Bolts of Asset-Based Finance

For example, if an auto financing company fails, asset-based investors would still receive cash flows from any loans they own, which sit in a separate legal entity. And the cars would continue to serve as collateral.

Tapping into a Varied Opportunity Set

The opportunity set is vast, particularly in the United States and Europe, where private asset-based credit is funding multiple forms of consumer spending and helping to address structural housing shortages.

The flexibility of the asset class provides an array of tools with which managers can shape cash flows from a wide range of income-generating assets into steady and diversified income streams with low correlation to other strategies—and to each other. The goal is to engineer a portfolio that generates a steady, curated set of cash flows throughout an economic cycle.

Access to various types of lending allows managers to tilt exposure across the span of the cycle as growth, credit availability and borrower behavior changes. This might mean leaning into subprime auto loans early in the cycle when access to credit is expanding but tilting toward residential mortgages and other seasoned assets in the later stages.

A Strategy Adaptable to the Economic Cycle

Most investments are backed by amortizing assets with relatively short average-weighted lives. That’s important because it removes the need to try to call the economic cycle before committing capital. Even in the late stages of a credit cycle, conditions typically weaken gradually.

This doesn’t mean that lenders ignore the cycle entirely. But the granularity and short-dated nature of these loans have the potential to act as a built-in buffer, allowing underwriting criteria to be adjusted quickly in a constant feedback loop.

For example, a pool of consumer loans originated in March may be underwritten with return expectations based on the performance of thousands of similar existing loans. If the economic outlook changes in April enough to alter likely performance, the underwriting standards lenders apply to the next pool of loans can be adapted. This enables continued investment throughout the cycle, and the short-term nature of most loans means they repay quickly.

In our view, this makes asset-based lending a strong complement to the direct-lending strategies that dominate many private credit allocations. Most privately originated loans to middle-market companies come with five-to-seven-year maturities designed to match the typical hold periods of the borrowers’ private-equity owners. It isn’t uncommon for several loans to require refinancing at or around the same time.

Control Through Deal Structuring

Other types of deal structuring can also play important roles. For example, portfolios are often structured to be overcollateralized. This means the total value of the cash flow the underlying loans generate is greater than the amount of debt issued against those assets.

Incentive structures for the originator also help to reduce risk. A transaction might be built so that the originators of a pool of auto loans only receive some or all of their fees after investors have received interest and principal. Another might require the originator to absorb losses first, a potentially valuable feature in the later stages of an economic cycle.

On the other hand, a lower-risk pool of assets, such as seasoned prime residential mortgages with low loan-to-value ratios, might provide an opportunity for investors to lean in the other direction by taking an equity position in the capital structure to boost return potential.

None of this means asset-based finance is immune to volatility or losses. But the granular nature of lending and the levers managers can pull to generate continuous amortizing cash flows may generate strong return potential in all market environments while diversifying exposure to other forms of public and private credit.

By focusing on how cash flows are created and prioritized, asset-based strategies allow investors to think about diversification as a design exercise, not just an allocation choice.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Boston Scientific: From Hypergrowth To Durable Compounding
data.description:
Boston Scientific (BSX), maker of popular medical devices such as WATCHMAN, has been punished by the market over the last six months, with stock down more than 35%. This is partly due to rerating as growth is slowing from its peak and starting to normalize. As a result, multiples versus growth expectations are being recalibrated. However, the business is still enjoying sustained double-digit growth and operating margins of 28%, and the selloff may be out of proportion compared to the slowdown. The company offers higher-than-peer growth at a fair valuation. With several catalysts in the pipeline, including WATCHMAN CHAMPION, I rate Boston Scientific a Buy.

Growth Profile: Deceleration, Not Breakdown

Management was justifiably happy with the numbers for FY and Q4 2025. In the words of Michael Mahoney, “We're super pleased with the quarter and the full year.” Full-year revenue came in at $20 billion, with operational revenue growth of 19.2% and organic revenue growth of 15.8%. For Q4, operational revenue was up 14.3%, at the higher end of guidance. With acquisitions contributing 160 basis points, organic revenue growth for the quarter came in at 12.7%. Importantly, revenue growth is substantially above pre-COVID levels, with revenue growth of 9.3% in 2019.

Full-year adjusted EPS of $3.06 topped guidance of $3.02 to $3.04, representing a 22% increase, on top of a 100 basis point improvement in adjusted operating margin to 28%. Electrophysiology grew by 35% in Q4, followed by WATCHMAN, which grew by 29% and is seeing increased international adoption. For full-year 2026, the company is guiding towards 10.5-11.5%, which does mark a legitimate slowdown. Full-year adjusted EPS at the midpoint of $3.46 would mark an increase of 13%, indicating a modest margin expansion and generally in line with our thesis of normalization.

Valuation Reset Creates Relative Opportunity

The 35% drop over the last six months has created a compelling multiple situation looking ahead. According to Seeking Alpha figures, analysts are estimating EPS of $3.91 in 2027 and $4.41 in 2028, both representing year-over-year growth of around 12.7%. This results in a forward P/E of 16.1x for 2027 and 14.3x for 2028, which can be considered underpriced if growth holds up.

Meanwhile, the market appears to be pricing Boston Scientific as a mid-growth company. At 18x FY1 earnings, it’s more expensive than Medtronic (MDT) and cheaper than Edwards Lifesciences (EW). Forward EV/EBITDA paints a similar picture, with BSC sitting at 15.4 versus Medtronic’s 12.8 and Edwards’ 20.4. However, look at the growth you’re buying at this price.

Boston Scientific’s 5-year revenue CAGR of 15.1% is vastly higher than its competitors at 4.9% and 6.7%, respectively. The same is true for the 3-year EBITDA CAGR, which at 19% towers above the competition, which is at 3.4% and 2.2%. Finally, the quality of the earnings is better, with a 3-year CAGR FCF of 41%, again considerably better than MDT’s 8.5% and EW’s 14.6%. Essentially, you’re paying a mid-tier valuation for meaningfully higher growth.

Sustaining Above-Market Growth

The question now becomes whether BSX is able to sustain this above-market growth rate, and there are several reasons to believe this is the case. Electrophysiology remains a structural growth driver, with Q4 growth of 35%, well above the market average of 18-20%. The market is expected to keep growing by 15% in 2026, and management has indicated they are confident that they can continue to outpace the overall market.

What could really drive growth is the WATCHMAN CHAMPION trial. WATCHMAN currently has an addressable market of around 5 million patients, but a successful expansion would quadruple this to 20 million patients. This would boost already strong growth of 29% and move the product from niche use to first-line therapy. However, it’s important to keep in mind that this is as of yet unproven.

Thirdly, the company is betting hard on M&A to enter into high-growth adjacent markets, and acquisitions like Penumbra and Valencia are already contributing meaningfully to the top line. As mentioned, acquisitions contributed 160 basis points in the most recent earnings report. Total cash and ST equivalents of $1.965 combined with net leverage of around 1.9 leaves room for further acquisitions.

In short, growth is not dependent on a single variable. EP, WATCHMAN, and segment expansion into other fields like oncology and M&A are all viable growth drivers, and together, they suggest that the company can at least keep up EPS growth estimates of around 11% per year as margins expand.

Risks & Catalysts

The primary risk to the Buy thesis is that revenue and EPS growth disappoint. This would mean that current forward multiples would start to look more optimistic. Specifically, if revenue and EPS growth drop into single digits, the normalization narrative would come under pressure, and structural decline would become a more accurate descriptor.

Also, already high margins leave less room for expansion, with operating margin already at 28%. The company’s dependency on EP and WATCHMAN means that execution here is key, and while diversification will help, investors will mostly be looking at the core business. If the results of the CHAMPION trial are unsuccessful, the addressable patient base will remain around 5 million.

This is also the potential upside. If trials are successful, this could lead to a massive revenue boost in a core business, which would help drive earnings growth as well. At the same time, recovery in weaker segments like urology, CRM, and neuromod would boost investor confidence. More news of M&A and specifically their contributions to the top line would also be a potential catalyst.

Conclusion

After a 35% drop in the past six months, Boston Scientific has undergone a significant multiple reset. Growth is clearly slowing down from peak levels, but it remains robust and durable. At current levels, the market seems to be pricing Boston Scientific as a mid-growth medtech company, but a closer look at the comps reveals that it's growing considerably faster than the competition. Essentially, Boston Scientific is transitioning from a hypergrowth company to a sustainable compounder, and it appears that the current valuation does not fully reflect earnings growth durability.

data.title: Dana Incorporated: Fundamentals Have Improved A Lot
data.description:
Investment Overview

I wrote about Dana Incorporated (DAN) previously with a buy rating in my initiation coverage, as I thought the business was in much better shape after the divestiture, had a path towards double-digit EBITDA margin, and also a de-risked near-term outlook. The developments since my last update have been largely positive. The balance sheet reset is done, margin improvement is now showing up in the numbers, and the growth story looks broader than before as ICE-related demand, backlog, and aftermarket momentum all improve. Therefore, I reiterate: buy.

My Old Bull Case Is Now Showing Up In The Numbers

There were two main points in my initiation coverage thesis: (1) the off-highway sale would fix the balance sheet, and (2) the cost program would lift margins. All of these are now visible in the numbers and in how management is framing 2026.

DAN finally closed the Off-Highway divestiture on 1st Jan this year, and it has already completed ~$2 billion of debt reduction. The latest cash position, as of the end of January, was $659 million, and liquidity was ~$1.8 billion. An important commentary made was that net leverage should stay <1x through 2026, which directly supports my view that this divestiture would fix the balance sheet. Next, the point on margin improvement also looks much more credible now if you look at the FY2025 numbers. While sales were down y/y to $7.5 billion, adj. EBITDA went up sharply to $610 million, with adj. EBITDA margin up to 8.1%. The numbers were even better if you just look at the Q4 numbers, in that adj. EBITDA margin reached 11.1% vs. 4.7% in Q4 2024. Note that this was driven mostly by cost savings and efficiency gains (as I said previously) and not entirely because of better volumes.

For the 2026 guidance, management guided to $7.3 billion to $7.7 billion of sales and $750 million to $850 million of adj. EBITDA. If you do the math, that implies a full-year adj. EBITDA margin of 10.6%, which tells us that this margin expansion story still relies largely on initiatives. And that also means that there is room for more expansion once revenue starts to scale up.

Growth Narrative

This part of the equity story has gotten much better as well. At the Capital Markets Day (CMD), management said only ~15% of quoting activity in 2023 was traditional or ICE-related because the industry was heavily tilted toward EV programs. Fast forward 3 years later, ~75% of current quote activity is ICE-related, and that is a significant swing. Management also said customers are revisiting product plans, extending ICE programs, and, in some cases, even launching new ICE vehicles. This goes against the narrative that “Electric Vehicles (EVs) will entirely displace ICE vehicles in the near term," and it matters a lot for DAN, as its legacy driveline, thermal, and sealing content is built for that kind of demand.

The base business growth profile also looks better. Specifically, Super Duty, which is DAN’s largest revenue program, has now been secured through 2038. Wrangler is also secured through 2029, and the JLR truck portfolio is secured even further through 2036. In other words, there is a very visible line of sight to the mid- and long-term growth, with potential upside when more ICE vehicle production is coming up. Then, you have the backlog to add more visibility to this growth narrative. DAN’s three-year new business backlog is now $750 million, up 33% y/y, and that is going to grow to $1.15 billion if we look through 2030.

Aftermarket

I would also add a stronger case that DAN can add growth in areas that make the growth story more durable and also with higher margins. The one area I would focus on is aftermarket. Per management, DAN’s global aftermarket business already generates more than $850 million of annual turnover, and that is split approximately 60/40 between Light Vehicle and Commercial Vehicle and is concentrated mainly in North America and EMEA. That may seem small now relative to the FY30 revenue target, but the growth runway here is huge. The estimate is that this addressable aftermarket market is worth ~$6.6 billion (per the CMD). I believe we would see strong growth here in the coming quarters/years, as, over the last six months, DAN has secured contracts with AutoZone, NAPA, and Advance Auto Parts. For reference, these three players represent >17,000 storefronts across the US and Canada.

Some might also think that this is not going to be a meaningful earnings growth driver given the size of the revenue, but remember, aftermarket usually carries a better margin profile and is less tied to new vehicle production. The 2030 guide provided was that the aftermarket can achieve a 32.5% adj. EBITDA margin, so even at this current $850 million, at that level of profitability, it translates to $276 million of adj. EBITDA, which would be close to 50% of FY2025 adj. EBITDA.

Valuation

After a solid share price rally since last August (when the stock was at ~14x NTM PE), I think the current valuation (~11x NTM PE) is another attractive entry point for investors. My new way of valuing DAN is by anchoring it against the FY30 target laid out at the CMD. In the CMD, management guided to $10 billion in revenue. From there, the question is what margins DAN would be able to achieve. DAN did achieve an adj. net margin of ~1.1% in FY25, and if we assume a high drop-through from adj. EBITDA margin expansion to the bottom line (assuming no step-up in leverage), which is about ~180 bps (management guided for a 10.7% adj. EBITDA margin vs. FY24 of 8.9%), we can bridge to DAN achieving ~3% or more adj. net margin by FY30 (assuming more fixed-cost leverage). Say that DAN exits at a 4% adj. net margin; that equates to FY30 adj. earnings of $400 million. Even without multiples expanding back to 14x, the stock is worth ~$4.4 billion market cap, or $40.

Risk

The risk is that the market may lose patience before DAN starts showing the strong earnings growth (i.e., wait till 2030), since the 2026 guide still assumes roughly flat sales. While I am bullish on the cost savings side of things, that also means there is limited potential for a sharp earnings growth acceleration in 2026 and maybe even early 2027. In the worst case, if DAN faces an unexpected cost increase and margins decline, the market may stop giving credit for the FY30 framework.

Conclusion

I maintain a buy rating on DAN. Fast forward to today, the balance sheet has already been repaired, margin expansion is already visible, and the growth drivers are more visible in that DAN now has multiple ways to grow earnings from here. I believe this makes the current setup attractive.

data.title: Invesco Global Focus Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 The fund lagged its benchmark for the quarter but exceeded peers.

Class A shares underperformed the MSCI ACWI Growth Index for the quarter but exceeded the peer group average, ranking in the 40 th percentile (147 of 314) of the Morningstar Global Large-Stock Growth category.

2 US stocks lagged non-US stocks; growth stocks underperformed

Global equities rose, led by emerging market and European equities, as Asia's technology-driven gains offset late-quarter weakness in US technology stocks.

3 Positioned for upside in 2026

Looking to 2026, we believe a supportive macroeconomic backdrop and easing monetary policy support our constructive outlook for global equities. We believe the most compelling opportunities are in high quality companies and leading AI innovators.

Manager perspective and outlook

  • Global equities were positive for the quarter, with international equities outperforming the US as late-quarter concerns about elevated technology valuations appeared to temper AI-driven momentum. Emerging markets led due to a broad Asian technology rally and robust results in South Korea; China lagged due to weak economic data and currency strength. Europe also delivered solid returns, supported by a stronger euro, improving manufacturing trends and expectations of fiscal stimulus in Germany.
  • Looking to 2026, we maintain a constructive outlook for global equities, supported by reduced trade policy uncertainty, firmer growth forecasts and expected monetary easing led by the US Federal Reserve, despite cooling in the US labor market. Risks such as slower growth, renewed inflation and geopolitics have persisted, but we do not expect them to materially alter our long-term investment views.
  • In this environment, we still favor high quality companies with good balance sheets, attractive profit margins and solid cash-generation capabilities. We see greater vulnerabilities in private markets and lower quality AI-themed companies reliant on debt-funded spending. We believe public markets will keep rewarding data center hyperscalers and AI innovators able to self-fund investment, with the most compelling long-term opportunities among companies owning leading semiconductor intellectual property or advanced large language models.

As of 12/31/25. Holdings are subject to change and are not buy/sell recommendations.

Portfolio positioning

Invesco Global Focus Fund is a high conviction portfolio with a bottom-up approach to stock selection. We believe consistent, long-term outperformance can best be achieved by investing within select long-term thematic trends, or tailwinds. A growing ecosystem with some advantaged participants is a good starting point for security level research, in our view, and that is where we direct our efforts.

We initiated one new position during the quarter.

Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) is a leading medical device company providing robotic-assisted surgery systems. Intuitive is most known for the da Vinci Surgical System that enables surgeons to perform minimally invasive procedures with enhanced precision, vision and control. We believe Intuitive offers a compelling value proposition at the intersection of cutting-edge medical technology and the needs of an aging global population.

We sold three positions during the quarter.

Zoetis (ZTS) specializes in animal health. We sold the position after management reported weak quarterly results and attributed the shortfall to a challenging macroeconomic environment and fewer veterinary visits. Competitors have not experienced similar issues, which makes us skeptical of the explanation. After Zoetis lowered its annual revenue forecast due to muted demand, we redeployed the capital into investments we believe offer more compelling return profiles.

Ferrari (RACE) is an Italian luxury car manufacturer. We sold the position after a meeting with management. We believe Ferrari has focused too much on personalization and hybrid vehicles, which historically tend to have lower resale values and, consequently, weaker demand. Management has since identified this issue and has been moving to scale back hybrids in favor of internal combustion engine cars but is not expecting much growth during the transition.

NiCE (NICE) is an enterprise software company serving customer engagement, financial crime and compliance markets. Management recently issued a weaker profitability forecast for 2026, citing heavy investment in AI and declines in revenue and net income. Also, NiCE has been considering the sale of Actimize, its profitable financial crime and compliance division, to help fund its acquisition of AI startup Cognigy, a move investors appear to have viewed cautiously. We are also increasingly concerned that NiCE's business is vulnerable to AI disruption, particularly from Anthropic (not a fund holding). Given these headwinds, we sold the position during the quarter.

Performance highlights

An overweight in health care, stock selection in financials and an underweight in information technology added to relative return.

The fund underperformed most in communication services and industrials due to stock selection and in consumer discretionary due to stock selection and an overweight.

Contributors to performance

Below are the largest contributors to relative return for the quarter:

Microsoft stock declined on concerns about rising AI capital spending and slower product uptake. The fund is underweight relative to the benchmark because we remain cautious about its valuation despite AI investment capacity. The underweight added to relative return.

Alphabet had a strong quarter with revenue topping $100 billion, driven by AI demand, Google Cloud growth and momentum in search advertising. TPU (tensor processing unit) chip sales and the success of Gemini Pro 3 in our view reinforced Alphabet's AI leadership and it remains the fund's largest holding.

Thermo Fisher Scientific shares rose following a better-than-expected quarterly earnings report in which it raised revenue forecasts. Recent acquisitions and strategic collaborations highlighted in our view the company's improved positioning moving forward.

Eli Lilly had a robust quarter due to soaring demand for GLP-1 obesity drugs. Management raised guidance and the stock reached a $1 trillion market cap. Amid growing optimism for its upcoming oral GLP-1 drug, we trimmed the position after strong gains.

Lam Research beat revenue and earnings expectations due to strong demand for advanced semiconductor tools as its etch and deposition technologies gained traction across next-generation chip designs.

Detractors from performance

Below are the largest detractors from relative return for the quarter:

Meta Platform stock fell on concerns about AI spending and a one-time tax charge, though we believe returns on AI investment remain strong. Our thesis is intact, but we trimmed the position in an effort to balance risk and reward.

Uber beat earnings estimates during the quarter, but operating income was below expectations. Communication regarding updated profit margin guidance also appeared to create some confusion.

ServiceNow faced industry headwinds and a government shutdown during the quarter. Its acquisition of Veza bolstered its AI portfolio but created concern about integration risk.

Tencent shares experienced volatility despite good quarterly earnings. Like many Chinese equities, the company faced tariff-related headwinds. We believe Tencent has demonstrated its ability to generate attractive returns on AI capital spending.

data.title: The $197 Billion Question: How The Mega IPO Wave Reshapes Capital Markets And Crypto
data.description:
The approaching mega-IPO cycle will function as a transient liquidity absorber across risk assets before post-lockup wealth recycling produces a net positive medium-term impulse for both equities and crypto.

$197 Billion Capital Has to Come From Somewhere

In 2025, the U.S. IPO market generated $44B in total proceeds, and the past 10-year total U.S. IPO capital raise is $469B. SpaceX (SPACE) is currently targeting a valuation of approximately $1.75T, with an expected raise of $50B-$70B. Adding OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO), both of which have expressed the intention to go public this year, the aggregate capital demand reaches $104-$197B. This estimated raising amount is already assuming an initial float of around 5-10%, well below the standard of 15% of typical IPO.

IPO norms typically involve offering 15-25% of shares to public markets, balancing liquidity for price discovery with continued control for founders and early investors. For example, Facebook (META) floated 15% and Google floated 19%. However, the total capital demand for SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic would be over $400B assuming a standard 15% float, an amount almost approaching the entire US IPO market raised amounts from 2016 to 2025 combined. This has made a 15% float just impossible. Initial floats for these companies will likely be significantly smaller, with 5-10% as a realistic baseline.

The first direct impact is the squeeze on smaller offerings this year as the market capacity is limited and selective. Karken has already paused its IPO plans until conditions improve, an early indicator of the crowding-out effect.

Capital directed toward mega-IPO allocations compresses valuations for smaller-cap growth equities, Bitcoin, and crypto broadly, as investors need to fund subscriptions. In addition to capital, culturally prominent listings also absorb financial media coverage and social trading activity for retail, suppressing performance in assets competing for the same marginal buyers.

Do Mega-IPOs Signal a Market Top?

A common view holds that massive public listings signal peak euphoria, as companies and their venture investors rush to go public when sentiment is elevated and valuations are stretched. This argument has some merit: U.S. equity markets remain richly valued even after the recent adjustment related to the Iran war.

The S&P 500 Cyclically Adjusted Price-to-Earnings (CAPE) ratio remains at historically high levels. This metric offers a more comprehensive view of valuation, as it adjusts for inflation and smooth earnings over a 10-year period.

The historical record for whether mega-IPO signal market top is, however, mixed. AT&T (T) in 2000 and Rivian in 2021 each landed near genuine market peaks, the two clearest "yes" cases. But Facebook and Alibaba (BABA) were each followed by continued market gains. The pattern is that mega-IPO is a symptom of high valuation in the market, a late-cycle sentiment gauge, but not a precise bell marking the market top.

This year's mega-IPO wave has an additional complicating factor: the current market environment is one of the rotation, not euphoria. In euphoria, all assets rise together, P/E multiples expand everywhere, and risk-on conditions are uniform across equities and crypto.

The current situation is clearly different. Some assets are making new highs while others are already in bear markets, multiple compress in crowded trades, and selectively replace indiscriminate buying.

The rotation has direction. Capital is moving out of two categories: assets disrupted by AI, high-valuation software where AI is compressing margins and displacing incumbents, and AI-adjacent names that accumulated large gains over 2023–2025 and now require earnings to justify their multiples. It is moving into assets with physical bottlenecks, where demand is structural and supply is constrained.

Energy is the clearest beneficiary. The current Iran conflict has added a geopolitical risk premium to oil. Beyond energy, basic materials, utilities, and industrials are each benefiting from a convergence of supporting factors that have been absent for most of the past decade. Geopolitically, the U.S. is actively rebuilding its industrial base, reshoring and defense spending are directing capital toward domestic manufacturing capacity. AI development requires physical inputs at scale: copper, steel, electrical transformers, and grid infrastructure. These sectors have structurally underperformed technology for years; the conditions that drove that underperformance are now reversing.

The right frame for the overall market is therefore not a broad top but a regime change within equities. Sectors disrupted by AI continue to suffer, IGV is down 23.5% year-to-date. Prior AI listings need to digest their 2023–2025 runs before the next leg. Sectors exposed to physical bottlenecks, energy, materials, utilities, industrials, are in the early stages of a multi-year re-rating. The overall market holds up, but the distribution of returns shifts significantly beneath the surface.

What Does This Mean for Bitcoin?

The impact of these mega-IPOs on Bitcoin is a liquidity story.

Since the spot ETF approvals in January 2024, Bitcoin ETFs have become a significant channel for institutional capital inflows into BTC. Because these ETFs trade within the U.S. equity market, they draw from the same capital pool as mega-IPO allocations. ETF flow data is therefore the most direct indicator of liquidity pressure on Bitcoin during the issuance window.

With the three companies targeting up to $197B in combined capital raises, available market liquidity will face measurable compression. During peak IPO windows, the base case for Bitcoin is trading range-bound, with net ETF flows as the key indicator to watch.

Post-Lockup Recycling Flow

The impact on Bitcoin can reverse materially once lockup periods expire. No S-1 filings have been made by any of the three companies as of late March 2026, so no lockup terms have been formally disclosed. Standard U.S. tech IPO practice is a 180-day lockup from the IPO date. Assuming listings occur in mid-to-late 2026, employee and VC liquidity would begin unlocking in early-to-mid 2027.

That capital historically recycles into venture, angel investing, and speculative assets at above-average rates. The crypto allocation within that recycling is likely higher than in prior tech liquidity cycles. SpaceX holds Bitcoin on its balance sheet, a disclosed treasury position that signals institutional comfort with BTC at the company level. Sam Altman's World Network (formerly Worldcoin) creates a separate but related crypto linkage from the OpenAI ecosystem.

data.title: John Hancock U.S. Global Leaders Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • U.S. stocks ended another strong year of performance on a positive note, advancing in the fourth quarter amid robust corporate earnings and two interest cuts by the U.S. Federal Reserve.
  • Value stocks outperformed growth shares in the fourth quarter, a notable change from the prior two quarters.
  • The fund underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 1000 Growth Index, due in part to stock selection in the IT sector.

Market review and outlook

The U.S. stock market remained on an upward trajectory in the fourth quarter, capping a third consecutive year of double-digit gains for the major U.S. equity indexes. Although momentum stocks continued to lead the market's overall advance as investor enthusiasm about AI remained high, market leadership broadened over the last half of the quarter. Sectors such as healthcare, industrials, and financials were among the better performers for the quarter. Nonetheless, IT stocks, which comprise more than a third of the S&P 500 Index and more than half of the Russell 1000 Growth Index, also fared well during the quarter.

2025 represented one of the most extreme momentum markets of the past seven decades, with market leadership concentrated in lower-quality, more speculative, and economically sensitive stocks. It isn't surprising, therefore, that the year was challenging for our quality growth strategy, which is focused on identifying and owning high-quality companies that offer reliable and durable growth regardless of the economic environment. However, we're heartened by the resilient growth opportunities across the fund's holdings and the historically attractive relative valuation of the portfolio. We believe the fund is well-positioned to benefit from continued broadening of market leadership and a softening of the extreme momentum dynamics that have dominated U.S. markets over the past few years.

Contributors and detractors

The fund posted a modestly positive return and trailed the performance of its benchmark. While the fund's relative performance improved over the past two quarters, the continued outperformance of lower-quality and speculative stocks remained a performance headwind.

Stock selection in the IT and communication services sectors also detracted from performance versus the index. Notable individual detractors included semiconductor manufacturer ARM Holdings PLC (ARM) and workflow management software company ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW) Despite a solid earnings report, ARM Holdings' shares sold off amid concerns about elevated research and development spending, while ServiceNow's shares declined amid investor concern over a new acquisition.

On the positive side, leading individual performers in the portfolio included medical products maker Cooper Companies, Inc. (COO), and customer relationship management software company Salesforce.com, Inc. (CRM) Cooper Companies reported strong earnings results during the quarter, while Salesforce benefited from better-than-expected subscription revenue growth.

Portfolio changes

In the fourth quarter, we added one new position—semiconductor manufacturer Broadcom, Inc. (AVGO), which has a business model supported by strong pricing power, recurring revenues, and promising growth opportunities. We didn't eliminate any portfolio holdings during the quarter.

data.title: Boeing Vs. Airbus: The Iran War Shock And The Production Reality
data.description:
Airplane manufacturers The Boeing Company (BA) and Airbus SE (EADSF) saw their share prices drop following the war in Iran, which cast a shadow on the economic growth that underpins demand for air travel and subsequently new airplanes. Boeing stock has lost nearly 17% since the invasion and 3% since my last report, in which I discussed the elements that come into play for airplane supply and demand. The European counterpart, Airbus, has lost 20% since the military action in the Middle East and lost 6.5% since my last report.

In this report, I analyze the airplane orders and deliveries for the first two months of the year (March figures are published in April). As mentioned in prior analyses, we're uniquely positioned to provide value estimates on orders and deliveries to more closely reflect the actual benefit to the businesses. While the current stock price performance is predominated by the developments in the Middle East, it remains of utmost importance to assess the production recovery process of Boeing and Airbus programs, as this has been the main bottleneck. At the same time, from the development of airplane orders, we will also be able to see in the months ahead whether there is any softening in order inflow. So, we are seeing that assessing the orders is going to carry more weight.

Below, I have attached the explanation of the analysis methodology from a prior report:

Explanation Of Analysis Methodology For Airplane Orders And Deliveries

For this analysis, I use an internally developed tool available on The Aerospace Forum. I will be assessing the net orders and deliveries. For each, I will discuss the units ordered and delivered and the associated dollar value.

The net orders provide us with a view of demand. This is a reflection of factors such as pricing and availability. Boeing and Airbus have a total backlog of nearly 15,000 airplanes. So, currently, we're not viewing things through the scope of needing more orders to support or increase production. The fact is that the backlogs have to be brought down to healthier levels, and that can be achieved through higher deliveries. Deliveries are what matter, and that's also the area where we see that neither Boeing nor Airbus has been able to support demand. Assessing the deliveries gives us a view of how the recovery for both manufacturers is pacing. As we assess the broader in-year trends, this report considers the year-to-date figures rather than monthly figures on orders and deliveries.

Boeing Extends Lead Over Airbus By Dollar Value

For the first two months of the year, Boeing and Airbus booked a total of 195 net orders, with Boeing collecting 118 net orders compared to 77 for Airbus, giving the US jet maker a 41-unit advantage. Boeing’s net order tally increased by just one order in the single-aisle airplane category, while net orders in the wide-body category increased by 14 units month-on-month. Airbus, on the other hand, booked 28 net orders, taking the lead in the single-aisle category, but did not receive any wide-body orders. So, Airbus currently trails in the wide-body category by 45 units.

In terms of net order value, Boeing’s lead increased from $6.1 billion to $6.6 billion, driven by strength in wide-body order inflow, bringing the net order value for the US jet maker to $10.9 billion, while Airbus increased the net order value by $1.6 billion to $4.3 billion. In the single-aisle category, Airbus leads by $0.4 billion, but the wide-body lead of $7 billion more than offsets that.

Airbus And Boeing Book Net Airplane Orders Value At Highest Level In Years

The net orders for Boeing and Airbus hit 195, marking an 84% increase in net orders, and are at the highest level after the pandemic. We note that net orders have been for the second consecutive year, with Airbus orders growing 18.5% and Boeing orders rising 188%. This gives Boeing a 61% share in the net orders, with the remaining 39% going to its European competitor.

In terms of value, the two jet makers have a combined net order total of $15.2 billion, which marks the highest level recorded since 2019. Just like with the net orders, we see that it is the second consecutive year of increasing net order value, but we note that the mix is in Boeing’s benefit. Airbus’ net order value dropped $2 billion, or 32%, while Boeing’s net order value rose $8.8 billion to $10.9 billion, driven by a strong order mix. This gives Boeing a 72% share in the net order value against 28% for Airbus.

Boeing Remains The Top Producer As Airbus Starts Weak

The first quarter of the year is generally a slow quarter for airplane deliveries, as jet makers try to meet end-of-year delivery deadlines in December, which, combined with the holidays, leaves a small inventory to lean on in the first quarter of the year. Nonetheless, we do see something interesting, and that is while Airbus seemingly has the highest production rates, it is Boeing that is delivering more airplanes even when its airplane inventory of pre-built jets has largely depleted. Boeing and Airbus delivered a total of 151 airplanes in the first two months of the year, with Boeing delivering 97 airplanes and Airbus 54 airplanes. In the single-aisle category, Boeing delivered 80 airplanes, leading Airbus’ 51 single-aisle deliveries by 29 units. In the widebody category, Boeing has a total of 17 deliveries, giving it a lead of 14 units over Airbus and a total lead of 43 airplanes. It is definitely a lead that can still slip, and in March there is the expectation that Boeing 737 MAX deliveries will be soft due to the correction of wiring issues.

In terms of value, Boeing delivered $6.6 billion worth of airplanes compared to $3.2 billion for Airbus, giving it a $3.4 billion lead. $2 billion of the lead is generated in the wide-body segment, while approximately $1.5 billion is generated in the single-aisle segment.

Airplane Production Shows Stability Rather Than Growth But Boeing Is Growing

At a time when airplane manufacturers are looking to rebuild production to record levels, we are seeing a rather unsettling reality, and that reality is that year-on-year deliveries have remained stable and are, in fact, marginally lower. Boeing is on an acceptable track with deliveries up 9% and at the highest level since 2019, but Airbus is really struggling with deliveries down 17% and at the levels last seen in 2021. Airbus’ main issue right now is engine deliveries due to the Pratt & Whitney GTF engine issues, which has led to the prioritization of getting grounded airplanes back in service. The result is that Boeing has a 64% share in the deliveries compared to 36% for Airbus.

In terms of value, both jet makers had a combined delivery output of $9.8 billion, which was down $0.3 billion year-on-year and in line with the first two months of 2020. Boeing grew delivery value by 6.5%, or $0.4 billion, to $6.6 billion, but Airbus’ delivery value slumped 18% to $3.2 billion and now stands slightly above the levels seen in 2021. Boeing has a 67% share in the delivery value compared to 33% for Airbus.

Boeing seems to be catching up with the shortfall in deliveries, having fallen from 50 units to 28 units. Airbus has been having issues consistently improving delivery numbers over the past years, but they seem to be around 35 units behind on what would fit the trend.

Conclusion: Boeing Is Doing Better; Airbus Is Having The Bigger Struggles

At this point, I do believe that Boeing is showing better consistency in production output. There will be a headwind in March, which could help Airbus close the gap somewhat in terms of deliveries, but generally Airbus has been on an extremely underwhelming path to increase production for the past two to three years. The result is that the engineering crisis that Boeing went through has not been as bad as it could have been since Airbus was simply not able to ramp up production as fast as intended. In terms of backlog, both airplane manufacturers have years of production secured, so that is not a major concern. However, at this point airplane manufacturing stocks seem to be a battle of losers, as investors are weighing down valuations of industrials, including aerospace and defense names, to account for a possible macroeconomic softening. While I do believe that is a net negative for Airbus and Boeing, it does give both manufacturers the ability to have a better balance between supply and demand. That is not necessarily a net positive, as it could soften pricing power somewhat. At the same time, we note that jet makers have seen their prices rebound strongly after each and every shock experienced. So, I do remain bullish on the prospects of both jet makers in the long term.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SCHH: It's Not A Hedge Against Push Inflation
data.description:
It's time to revisit the Schwab US REIT ETF (SCHH). I previously covered the ETF in February when I augmented an educational article. Unlike February's coverage, today's article communicated a directional outlook. More specifically, I caution against the expectation that SCHH will provide a hedge against a push inflation-based rally; let's traverse into the analysis.

A Couple Of Starting Points

SCHH In A Nutshell

For those unaware of SCHH, it is a U.S.-based equity REIT ETF. In its own words, “The fund’s goal is to track as closely as possible, before fees and expenses, the total return of an index composed of U.S. real estate investment trusts classified as equities,” which simply means the REIT provides investors with systematic access to U.S. equity REITs. In terms of its construction, SCHH follows a float-adjusted market cap weighting scheme and caps its single security exposure at 10%. Moreover, the ETF avoids illiquid issuers by mandating a focus on REITs with market capitalisations above $200 million.

A glance at SCHH ETF's portfolio (Figure 1) shows that its primary sector exposure is health care, retail, and industrial. However, material exposure to telecommunications, residential, data centres, self-storage, and other specialised REITs is also evident—the other data points shown in Figure 1 are zoomed in on later in the article.

In terms of its historical performance. A since-inception chart shows that SCHH has underperformed large-cap U.S. equities (illustrated by the following diagram) since its vintage. Moreover, as illustrated later in the article, cyclicality is evident, making position sizing and timing highly important.

In a nutshell, SCHH provides liquid access to U.S. equity REITs with a slight tilt towards healthcare, industrial, retail, and multifamily REITs. The ETF has underperformed U.S. large-cap equities by a staggering amount since its vintage.

Push vs. Pull Inflation

I often see analysts say, “Inflation is good for real estate,” which is a statement I find baffling, as it treats inflation as homogeneous. Whether inflation benefits real estate or not would depend on the source. For example, inflation can be broken down into demand-pull and cost-push inflation, whereby the former refers to a healthy, strong demand-side economy causing higher price levels, and the latter refers to a supply-side shock, which leads to higher prices. Conventionally, the latter leads to a temporary increase in prices due to the stickiness of demand. However, ultimately, we'll almost always see it cause weakness in the demand side, leading to recession risk (or at least instability among consumers).

Where are we today? Price levels have yet to escalate post-U.S.-Iran. However, I think we are in cost-push territory; I've assumed my stance for three reasons. Firstly, the U.S.-Iran war has led to persistently higher global oil prices and broad-based seaborne supply chain disruptions, which could soon filter into both the producers' and consumers' price index. Furthermore, we have entered an era of global trade fragmentation and potential deglobalisation, which could result in structurally higher input cost bases due to lower emphasis on absolute advantages, relative advantages, and offshoring of labour. Lastly, I think the consumer cycle was on a knife-edge pre-U.S.-Iran—consumer sentiment had already waned significantly. I believe consumers were kept at bay by the Federal Reserve's rate cuts and resilient unemployment numbers, created by residual benefits of a growth in services-based labour opportunities pre- and post-pandemic.

Why SCHH Isn't A Push Inflation Hedge

The Early Signs Are There

SCHH has shed ~7.92% month-over-month, showing that investors essentially priced it as a positive interest rate duration asset. Why would this have occurred? In my view, likely due to investors pricing the possibility of higher interest rates and demand-side weakness, the phenomenon translated into the perception of REITs' asset-liability management (higher liability level, slower rental escalation).

Post-pandemic history shows that a large increase in push-side inflation (2022) was transmitted into a subsequent decline of SCHH's market price. In addition, post-2022's inflation-calming period saw SCHH's price recover, extended by rate cuts and an improved consumer demand side.

Leverage

REITs are leveraged vehicles. For example, if we look at SCHH's three largest constituents, Welltower Inc. (WELL), Prologis, Inc. (PLD), and Equinix, we see Net Debt/EBITDA ratios of 3.03x, 5.3x, and 3.8x. While large REITs usually cover their leverage ratios quite well, their liability levels can reprice if nominal interest rates increase and credit spreads expand (either through floating-rate adjustments or the need for new issuances when existing debt expires). Investors will usually price the prospects of higher financing rates in advance. Although rents can escalate alongside inflation, escalation typically occurs more slowly, and push-side inflation leaves less scope for REITs to escalate without increasing vacancy rates.

Consumer and Industrial Cycle-Dependent

Retail and Industrial REITs can start suffering if the consumer and industrial cycles start deteriorating as a consequence of push-side inflation. In fact, variables such as consumer sentiment already started waning before the U.S.-Iran event, suggesting that headwinds would've occurred in any case.

To supplement the above, I cite the fact that SCHH's top retail/diversified REIT, Realty Income Corporation (O), had recently softened its full-year same-store rental escalation outlook to 1.15% (Figure 5), slightly below the 1.30% achieved in 2025. In addition, it framed a lower occupancy rate of 98.5% (from 98.9% in 2025). Although O REIT is merely one holding in SCHH's portfolio, it acts as a ballast among retail and diversified REITs, meaning its performance echoes the fundamental trajectory of its sectors. In addition to O's softer outlook, I believe industrial REITs will be hit especially hard if we see a continuation in push-side inflation, as will multifamily REITs, given that both are directly linked to the industrial/business cycle.

Side Note: The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS) places the odds of a recession at 30%, and Moody's Corporation (MCO) has assigned a 50% probability.

Financial Market Risk Premia

We often forget that REITs are financial assets, meaning their traded performance isn't 1:1 with fundamentals. Investors go through various risk regimes. A risk-off regime typically occurs when inflation is high and unstable, which can lead to a sell-off in risky assets such as REITs. As illustrated by the following figure, the S&P VIX Index (VIX) has been relatively subdued since the U.S. Federal Reserve started cutting interest rates in 2024. However, the instability of recent macro variables has led the VIX higher, arguably revealing complacency by risk-on investors. Assuming persistence in the geopolitical instability, we could see the VIX structurally higher for a while, leading to a spike in risk market premiums.

The Dividend Doesn't Provide Much Drawdown Coverage

Many invest in REITs for dividend safety. However, it should be noted that SCHH has a substantial historical max drawdown of 44.22%, suggesting a dividend won't meaningfully soften the blow if a left-tail risk event occurs via excessive push inflation.

Valuations Are Already Steep

SCHH's price-to-book ratio of 2.25x is already quite high for a REIT vehicle, considering the fact that REITs are priced on a tangible asset base. For a more granular view, I embedded SCHH's top 10 holdings' price/book ratios below. Some might disagree, but I think price-to-book ratios are cyclically high, given the strong run-up in REITs during the Federal Reserve's easing cycle—paired with a portfolio aggregate 5-year earnings growth rate of merely 4.35%, I fail to see the ETF's current price level being sustainable. I think the market might look for reasons to sell expensive REITs if a bear market narrative manifests.

Conclusion

I think the events in Iran will have a lasting impact on inflation based on higher oil prices, supply chain disruptions, and the general fragmentation of global trade. Although the Schwab US REIT ETF might hedge against demand-side inflation, its use case as a push inflation hedge is suboptimal. Moreover, SCHH's multiples are relatively high, industry leverage will be debated if bond yields stay pent-up, and the underlying earnings growth of SCHH's constituents is moderate.

I'm actively looking for opportunities within the oversold individual REITs sphere. However, I'm not going to engage with SCHH, especially not conditional on it being an inflation hedge.

data.title: Alaska Air Group: Double Downgrade On Oil Price And Macro Risk
data.description:
Alaska Air Group (Alaska Airlines) (ALK) stock is up 7.2% as the sentiment is largely positive on the broader market. The uptick in the share price comes a day after the company updated its guidance; while the release reads somewhat positively, it is not a positive adjustment to the expectations. In this report, I discuss the updated guidance and update my price target for Alaska Airlines, as I can no longer maintain the strong buy rating issued in October 2025.

Alaska Airlines Plagued by High Oil Prices

On the 30th of March, Alaska Airlines issued a guidance update, and while the company does highlight demand strength, I do believe the outlook is much more uncertain in the balance of the year. The company noted that demand continued to show strength, with unit revenues tracking in line with prior expectations. Into the second quarter, forward bookings 90 days out are up 25% for corporate travel, with yields and load factors also up.

The company did note that fuel costs have increased materially. Alaska Airlines always has some disadvantages when it comes to fuel costs, and the fuel price environment is now evolving in a negative way with refining margins increasing. The company now expects economic fuel prices to be $2.90-$3.30, providing a $0.70 headwind, with additional headwinds in the form of storms in Hawaii and cartel violence in Mexico leading to softer demand. The company noted that absent fuel and demand disruptions, the guidance would have been met at the midpoint of the range, but including disrupting factors, the company now expects a loss per share between $1.50 and $2.00. Previously, the company expected a loss per share of $0.50-$1.50. So, at the lower end there was a $0.50 downward shift, and at the higher end of the range, a $1.00 shift at the high end with a midpoint shift of $0.75, largely reflective of the oil price headwind.

On the demand and yield side, Alaska Airlines seems to be upbeat. However, we note that with unit revenues tracking as expected for Q1, they did not really absorb the higher fuel prices, which are typically partially passed through if the demand environment allows it. So, that increases pressure in the second quarter, and from those comments, it seems that demand is robust and yield is up. However, the company only quantified corporate travel demand trends

Analysts Have Revised Estimates Down

For Q1, analysts have revised their revenue estimates up by less than a percent to $3.32 billion, with EPS revised down from -$0.84 to -$1.05. For the second quarter, revenue estimates have been revised up by 1.3%, while EPS estimates have fallen by 35.2%. We do note that this decline has been consistent over the one-month, three-month, and six-month windows. It is not necessarily an indication that the war pressure is not fully priced in, but it is a risk. More importantly, the annual figures show that revenues are still expected to rise 9.3% with a 1.3% upward revision over the past month, but EPS estimates have been revised down by 20.6%. It gives the impression that there is extremely limited space for Alaska Airlines to pass through higher fuel costs, or at least a limited pass-through is being modeled right now. So, the risk remains that elevated fuel prices and associated macro risk may inflict further downside to the estimates.

Alaska Airlines is Undervalued, but for a Reason

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Alaska Airlines, I am adjusting my rating from strong buy to hold. We note that at the company’s 3-year median EV/EBITDA, the stock remains undervalued against the peer group, but right now we see major risk in the form of elevated oil prices and macroeconomic pressures that could affect demand this year and into the next year. So, while there is upside, I am downgrading the stock. We note that EBITDA estimates for 2026 have come down by 22%, and for 2027 estimates have remained stable, but there is downward pressure on those estimates driven by macroeconomic growth risks. So, if you want to buy this, it would be more of a contrarian buy, betting on the war ending over the next couple of days.

Margins are expected to dip this year and rebound in the next year, but whether that rebound will actually materialize will depend on the state of the global economy, which at this point is highly uncertain. Cash conversion remains highly irregular, driven by a combination of demand and timing of capital expenditures. The net debt leverage is expected to remain within an acceptable range this year, but the cash pile is set to decline, meaning that additional debt could be added.

Conclusion: Alaska Airlines is Not Looking Attractive

Solely looking at the current estimates, Alaska Airlines is looking somewhat attractive. However, I do believe that there is additional downward pressure on the earnings estimates for this year and the coming years, mostly driven by a potential softening of demand due to macroeconomic growth tapering. In terms of fuel prices, my expectation is that there is also additional risk there, primarily this year. Alaska Airlines does not hedge its fuel exposure. The company previously used crude oil call options to hedge fuel exposure (10K, Page 9), but all positions were settled in 2025 with no hedge positions by the end of 2025.

Given the combination of macroeconomic growth risks and the oil price environment, I do believe it is prudent to downgrade the stock to hold.
